topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email sat on the screen, stark white against the dark background of my laptop. My fingers hovered over the 'delete' button, then settled on the refresh icon, a pointless ritual. The words—'regret to inform you that your application has not been selected for the scholarship'—felt heavy, like a stone lodged beneath my ribs. I reread the rejection, focusing on the precise, sterile language, searching for some hidden loophole, some misplaced comma that might signify an error. My stomach tightened, a cold knot forming in my chest that made it hard to draw a full breath. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my room, the cheap carpet muffling my footsteps. Every time I stopped, I would stare at the ceiling, tracing the cracks in the plaster, my jaw clenched so tightly my molars ached. The silence of the apartment suddenly amplified the frantic drumming in my ears.

[story 2]

I kept glancing at the clock on the wall, the second hand seeming to drag through molasses. The official notification had arrived just before lunch, and I hadn't moved from my desk. My hands were clammy, slick with a sudden, inexplicable sweat despite the cool air conditioning. I picked up my pen, but the ink looked smeared, meaningless. I started methodically shuffling through the documents again, my eyes skipping over the carefully crafted essays, landing repeatedly on the final score summary. A low, shaky sound escaped my throat—a small, involuntary hitch of air. I pushed my chair back so sharply it scraped against the floorboards, and I walked to the window. Outside, the campus quad was bustling, full of students laughing and moving with purpose. I felt intensely isolated, a small, frozen statue observing a vibrant world I no longer felt connected to.

[story 3]

He read the rejection letter twice, slowly, deliberately, as if the words themselves held some hidden, poisonous meaning. His gaze flickered from the screen to the framed photo of his family on the shelf, then back again. A strange pressure built behind his eyes, a sensation like trying to hold back a tide. He didn't move to open a window or call anyone. Instead, he began to meticulously straighten the stack of textbooks on his desk, aligning the spines with painstaking precision. Each movement was rigid, controlled, a desperate attempt to impose order on the swirling chaos inside him. When he finally placed the last book perfectly straight, he leaned back in his chair, his shoulders slumping slightly, a deep weariness settling over him, as if the weight of the decision had physically settled onto his frame.
